title: [Bug]: pull --creds authentication not passed to registry via alias
body:
### Issue Description

I have configured a registry alias in `/etc/containers/registries.conf.d/aliases.conf`, and I'm trying to use it in `podman pull --creds user:pass`, but the authentication is not passed through. If I use the registry name directly then authentication succeeds.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Configure a registry alias, for example

```
[[registry]]
prefix = "xyx.com/prod"
location = "1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com"
```

2. Pull using this alias: `podman pull --log-level=debug --creds foo:bar xyz.com/prod/image:tag`


### Describe the results you received

Accessing the registry fails with an authentication error. The trace output shows that the alias was resolved but no credentials matched.

```
Trying to pull xyz.com/prod/image:tag...
DEBU[0000] Copying source image //xyz.com/prod/image:tag to destination image [overlay@/srv/containers+/run/containers/storage:overlay.mount_program=/usr/bin/fuse-overlayfs,overlay.mountopt=nodev]xyz.com/prod/image:tag 
DEBU[0000] Using registries.d directory /etc/containers/registries.d 
DEBU[0000] Trying to access "1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/image:tag" 
DEBU[0000] No credentials matching 1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/image found in /run/containers/0/auth.json 
DEBU[0000] No credentials matching 1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/image found in /root/.config/containers/auth.json 
DEBU[0000] No credentials matching 1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/image found in /root/.docker/config.json 
DEBU[0000] No credentials matching 1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/image found in /root/.dockercfg 
```

If I pull directly using the real registry name, authentication succeeds:

```
Trying to pull 1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/image:tag...
DEBU[0000] Copying source image //1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/image:tag to destination image [overlay@/srv/containers+/run/containers/storage:overlay.mount_program=/usr/bin/fuse-overlayfs,overlay.mountopt=nodev]1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/image:tag 
DEBU[0000] Using registries.d directory /etc/containers/registries.d 
DEBU[0000] Trying to access "1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/image:tag" 
DEBU[0000] Returning credentials for 1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/image from DockerAuthConfig 
```


### Describe the results you expected

The authentication should be passed through and applied against the alias.

### podman info output

comments.body: @vrothberg @mtrmac PTAL

```toml
[[registry]]
prefix = "xyx.com/prod"
location = "1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com"
```

That’s not quite an “alias”, in particular it’s not intended to be transparent for credentials.

E.g. this configuration can also point at read-only mirrors, and each mirror server might need a separate set of credentials.

Or it can be used to override (if not completely disable) references to public registries like the Docker Hub or Quay.io (“references to `docker.io` are all redirected to the company’s mirror that is restricted to approved software”). It would be _bad_ if users’ private credentials to such an internet registry were sent to a corporate registry instead.

Also note that the `--creds` option does not say _which_ registry the credentials are for: is it for`xyx.com`, or for the AWS one? Is that particular CLI user aware of the `registries.conf` configuration?

The recommended solution to all of that is to construct a special-purpose `auth.json` file carrying the credentials (because that file can contain more than one set of credentials, and the credentials are clearly associated with specific registries and namespaces), and to use `podman pull --authfile`.
@mtrmac how about c/image adds a debug log to make this choice more explicit/obvious?

The original report already quotes a debug log:
> ```
> DEBU[0000] No credentials matching 1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/image found in /run/containers/0/auth.json 
> ```

I tend to think that is sufficient. We could explicitly look for a case where there are no credentials for the destination location, but there _are_ credentials for the logical location (only for redirects, not for mirrors???), and add an extra debug log entry, but the above already seems sufficient to me to indicate what needs doing.

The above certainly does not explain _why_ the system is designed that way, the way https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/17185#issuecomment-1402118304 tries to do, but I’m doubtful we can fit that explanation into a log line.

I think that the current debug log leaves space for interpretation that there may be a bug. Something indicating that the "user-specified credentials are not used for mirror for security reasons" should fill this space.

Note that the credentials what were expected to be used come from `auth.json`; i.e. we would need to do an extra lookup to see if they exist. We did not actively ignore credentials from `SystemContext` (that code path also exists, and actually has no debug log, but it’s irrelevant to this report).

In the worst case, that extra lookup to see if there are credentials we didn’t use could trigger interactive prompts if credential helpers were involved. I guess that could be avoided by adding an extra “never be interactive” parameter to `GetCredentials`, or something. So it’s possible but fairly hairy.

> That’s not quite an “alias”, in particular it’s not intended to be transparent for credentials.

Fair enough.
Is there a way to have an alias, so that I can always refer to `1234567890ab.dkr.ecr.us-east-2.amazonaws.com` as `xyz.com/prod`, including credentials (and even on push)?
Not as a Podman feature, to my knowledge. I suppose a DNS CNAME could work, but not with TLS.

> Not as a Podman feature, to my knowledge. I suppose a DNS CNAME could work, but not with TLS.

There's some discussion at https://github.com/aws/containers-roadmap/issues/299 about using a reverse proxy or AWS CloudFront to provide a nicer name for AWS ECR registries, which I guess is the only solution for now then.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@hmoffatt @mtrmac @vrothberg what should we do with this issue?

I think the best idea that has come up so far is to detect this situation and to add a warning, but that warning couldn’t be reliable in presence of interactive credential helpers, and we would need new parameters and code paths to avoid referring to the credential helpers, just to warn about a situation that has, to my knowledge, happened once during the past 5 years redirection has existed  — so I think that’s not worth it, or at least I don’t plan to work on it.
Is there something that could be added to the documentation?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@mtrmac what do you think about documenting this issue?
I’m fairly skeptical that adding a sentence or two somewhere would make much of a difference in practice (see how long `containers-registries.conf(5)` is already); “PRs welcome” I guess.

I think the source of confusion is that passing `--creds` on the Podman CLI will be used solely for the entered source registry.  A sentence in Podman docs may be more helpful and easier to find than in `containers-registries.conf(5)` (which I agree will be buried in a gigantic man page).
Lets open a PR for podman * --creds man page and add it.

> Lets open a PR for podman * --creds man page and add it.

https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/18055
Thanks all, I think the PR is a good solution to my problem.
Oops, in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/17185#issuecomment-1406687487 I for some reason thought that the credentials in question come from `auth.json` and not `--creds`.
